content.full_text:
Farn Porrucel PnacrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
March 5,2004
Susan R. McKenzie, M.D.
Departnent of Industrial Relations
Division of Workers' Compensation
Medical Unit
Post Office Box 8888
San Francisco, California 94128
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-0+011
Dear Dr. McKenzie:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the honoraria
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').r Nothing in this letter should be
construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this Ietter is
based on the facts presented. The FairPolitical Practices Commission (the "Commission')
does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTTONS
(1) May employees ofthe Workers' Compensation Medical Unit who serve as
speakers in continuing education progmms accept reimbursexnent from course providers
for expenses such as travel, lodging, and appropriate meals?
(2) I|vday anployees ofthe Workers' Compensation Medical Unit who approve
course providers for continuing education programs also serve as speakers and accept
reimbursement from these course providers for expenses such as travel, lodging and
appropriate meals?
' Government Code sections 8 I 000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All references herein are to the Government Code
unless otherwise indicated.
-6-nE


File No. A-04-011
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
(1). Yes. The honoraria prohibition does not apply to actual intrastate
transportation and any necessary lodging and subsist€,nce expenses provided directly in
connection with a speech orparticipation in apanel or seminar.
(2). Yes. Actual intrastate kansportation and any necessary lodging and
subsistence expenses provided directly in connection with a speech orparticipation in a
panel or seminar are not income or gifts to the recipient and, therefore, do not qualifr as
an economic interest which could present a potential conflict of interest.
FACTS
You are a staffphysician with the Division of Workers' Compensation Medical
Unit ("DWC') in the Department of Industrial Relations. One of the division's mandates
is to oversee the qualified medical evaluatorprogram in which the division accredits
physicians to perform medicalJegal evaluations in the workers' compensation systern.
As part of that program, the division also approves providers of courses which these
physicians must take in order to fulfill their continuing educational requirernents
mandated bythe Labor Code.
Members of the DWC staffare occasionally asked to serve as speakers in these
continuing educational programs. There is a significant advantage to the system in having
DWC staffspeak since staffcan provide first-hand knowledge as to how the deparhnent's
programs are administered. The courses are given in Califomia
AIYALYSIS
QursrroxNo.l
Ifoxonmrrnrr
The DWC is an agency within the meaning of section 82003. Forpurposes of this
analysis, it is assumed that the employees who may be called upon to serve as speakers in
the above mentioned continuing educational programs are "designated employees" as
defined in section 82019.
Section 89502(c) states that no designated employee of an "agency shall accept an
honorarium from any source if the member or employee would be required to report the
receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic interests."
Section 89501(a) defines honorarium as "anypayment made in consideration for any
speech gtven, article published, or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering."


File No. A-04-011
Page No. 3
However, Title2,Division 6, California Code of Regulations2 sectionl8932.4(e)
states that "honorarium" does not include "actual intrastate transportation and any
necessary lodging and subsistence provided directlyin connection" with an event in
which the employee gives a speech orparticipates in apanel or seminar. Additionally,
these items are not payments and need not be reported by the employee.
Similarprovisions are found in regulation 189a2@)(ll) exempting such travel
expenses from the gift provisions of the Act and regulation 18950.1(a) and 18950.3 with
regard to payments made for travel pu{poses.
Accordingly, payment of actual and necessary travel expenses to employees who
participate as speakers at continuing educational courses in California is not prohibited by
the provisions of the Act banning honoraria. Likewisq such travel expenses are not gifts,
and are therefore not subject to the gift limits imposed by the Act.
Qursuox No.2
Coxrr,rct oT INTEREST
Your second question implicitly asks whether or not e,nrployees responsible for
approving course providers for continuing educational credits may also take part as
speakers in the courses presented by the course providers they approve without violating
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public o{ficials '\rill perfiorm
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias causedbytheir own financial interests or
the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).). Section
87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making or othe,nnise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official
has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifring conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700.) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever apublic official makes, participates
in making or otherwise uses his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his
or her financial interests.
Srrp I
PusI,rc Orrrcrnr,
Employees of DWC are public officials within the meaning of section 82048.
Consequently, an ernployee may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use his or
her official position to influence any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on any economic interest he or she may have.
2 AI1 regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califomia Code of Regulations, L nlsss
otherwise indicated.


FileNo. 4-04-011
Page No. 4
Srnr2
Mlxrxc, Pl,nrtcrp,lrrxc IN, oR rxrr,urxclxc l GovrnxurrrrA1, DECrsr61.I
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting within
the authority of his or her office orposition: obligates his orher agency to any course of
action, enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency, or determines
not to act, unless such determination is made because of his or her financial interest
(Regulation 18702.1 (a).) Under the facts prese,nted, the governmental decision herein is
whether or not to^approve applicants as course providers for continuing education under
DWC's program.3
Srrp3
PorsxrrAr,r,y DrseuAlrryrNc EcoNoMrc Ixrunnsrs
Since this question involves a determination of whether or not the travel expenses
constitute an economic interest, we will proced directly to the third step of the analysis.
Section 87103 states that *[a] public official has a financial interest... if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amaterial financial effec!
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family," or on any economic interest. The economic interests relevant to
your inquiry are:a
"(c) Any source of income ... aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or
more... received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made.
T...11
*(e) Any donor of ... a grft or gifts aggregating three hundred fofi dollars
($3+01 or more in value provided to, received by, orpromised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made-"
Section 82030 defines "income" as"apq/ment receivd, including but not limited
to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift,
including any glft of food orbeveragg loan, forgiveness orpayrnent ofindebtedness
received by the filer, reimbursement for expenseS, ptr diem, or contribution to an insurance
or pension program paid by any person other than an employer ...." (Emphasis added.)
Section 82028(a) defines "dft" as"any paymentthat confers apersonal benefit on
the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received ....
Any person . . . who claims that a payment is not a gift by reason of receipt of consideration
has the burden ofproving that the consideration received is of equal or greater value."
3 Please note, an official with a financial interest in a decision must also refrain from participating
in making or influencing the decision (regulations 18702.2,18702.3).
n Under the facts presented to us, these are the only potentially disqualifying economic interests,
and this analysis assumes there are no other economic interests involved.


File No. A-04-011
Page No. 5
However, regulation 18942 provides certain exceptions to the definition of gift. Nothing
delineated below is considered a gift, nor is it subject to any limitation on gifts:
"(11) Free admission, and refreshments and similarnon-cash
nominal benefits provided to a filer during the entire event at
which the filer gives a speech, participates in apanel or seminar, or
provides a similar servicg and actual intrastate transportation and
any necessary lodging and subsistence provided directly in
connection with the speech, panel, serninar, or sen ice, including
but not limited to meals and beverages on the day ofthe activity.
The items are not payments and need not be reported by any filer.,,
(Emphasis added.)
Similarly, regulations 18950.1 and 18950.3 provide the same exception to
payments made for travel purposes. Since the above Ianguage provides that these
reimbursements are not "pa5rments," we have advised that they are neither income, nor
gifts, under the Act. (Kintner Advice Letter, I-03-l0l; Benninghoven Adice Letter, I-98-
177).
Therefore, since the employees referred to in your second question are reeeiving
neither gifts nor income by collecting travel expenses, they have no economic interest
which could create a conflict of interest under the Act-
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
By: w
Lemkeit
Counsel, Legd Division
wJLjg

(2) I|vday anployees ofthe Workers' Compensation Medical Unit who approve
course providers for continuing education programs also serve as speakers and accept
reimbursement from these course providers for expenses such as travel, lodging and
appropriate meals?

unless otherwise indicated.
-6-nE

CONCLUSION: (1). Yes. The honoraria prohibition does not apply to actual intrastate

connection with a speech orparticipation in apanel or seminar.
(2). Yes. Actual intrastate kansportation and any necessary lodging and
subsistence expenses provided directly in connection with a speech orparticipation in a
panel or seminar are not income or gifts to the recipient and, therefore, do not qualifr as
an economic interest which could present a potential conflict of interest.
